--- SOURCE 3: CHCs Face Financial Struggles ---
URL: https://www.commonwealthfund.org/blog/2024/community-health-centers-are-serving-more-patients-ever-financial-challenges-loom-large

SUMMARY:
Community health centers (CHCs) are providing high-quality, comprehensive primary care to more patients than ever. To better understand their financial pressures, we analyzed data from the Commonwealth Fund 2024 Survey of Federally Qualified Health Centers, as well as new national community health center data. CHCs are funded through various revenue sources, including insurance reimbursements, federal grants and appropriations, and revenue from the 340B program, which allows safety-net providers to purchase drugs at discounted rates and use the savings to cover other services. The Community Health Center Fund, which provides 70 percent of CHCs’ federal funding, is set to expire at the end of 2024. Data from the Commonwealth Fund 2024 National Survey of Federally Qualified Health Centers reveal that in the coming years, many CHC leaders expect their financial challenges to persist or worsen.

FULL CONTENT:
Published Time: 2024-11-13

November 13, 2024

Community Health Centers Are Serving More Patients Than Ever, but Financial Challenges Loom Large
=================================================================================================

![Image 3: Overhead look at blood pressure being taken at fold up table](https://www.commonwealthfund.org/sites/default/files/styles/hero_image_desktop/public/Federman_CHC%20Primary%20Care_1722991766_3x2.png?h=4362216e&itok=YTErDku6)

Patients have their blood pressure and other vital signs checked at an intake triage in a remote area medical (RAM) mobile dental and medical clinic on October 7, 2023, in Grundy, Va. Community health centers provide essential primary care to over 30 million people, including those who are uninsured, have low incomes, or live in rural areas. Funding for these centers is not keeping up with rising costs, however. Photo: Spencer Platt/Getty Images

Patients have their blood pressure and other vital signs checked at an intake triage in a remote area medical (RAM) mobile dental and medical clinic on October 7, 2023, in Grundy, Va. Community health centers provide essential primary care to over 30 million people, including those who are uninsured, have low incomes, or live in rural areas. Funding for these centers is not keeping up with rising costs, however. Photo: Spencer Platt/Getty Images

Assistant Vice President, Delivery System Reform, The Commonwealth Fund 

[](https://www.facebook.com/sharer/sharer.php?u=https%3A%2F%2Fwww.commonwealthfund.org%2Fblog%2F2024%2Fcommunity-health-centers-are-serving-more-patients-ever-financial-challenges-loom-large)[](https://www.linkedin.com/shareArticle?mini=true&url=https://www.commonwealthfund.org/blog/2024/community-health-centers-are-serving-more-patients-ever-financial-challenges-loom-large)[](mailto:;?subject=Community%20Health%20Centers%20Are%20Serving%20More%20Patients%20Than%20Ever%2C%20but%20Financial%20Challenges%20Loom%20Large&body=https%3A%2F%2Fwww.commonwealthfund.org%2Fblog%2F2024%2Fcommunity-health-centers-are-serving-more-patients-ever-financial-challenges-loom-large)

Senior Program Assistant, Federal and State Health Policy, The Commonwealth Fund 
*   ![Image 9: Alexandra Bryan](https://www.commonwealthfund.org/sites/default/files/styles/bio_photo_thumb_160x160_/public/AlexandraBryan_1x1.jpg?itok=fcj3H02t)  **[Alexandra Bryan](https://www.commonwealthfund.org/person/alexandra-bryan)**
Former Senior Program Assistant, Delivery System Reform 
*   ![Image 10: Celli Horstman](https://www.commonwealthfund.org/sites/default/files/styles/bio_photo_thumb_160x160_/public/Celli-Hortsman_1x1.png?itok=qj9RAvUZ)  **[Celli Horstman](https://www.commonwealthfund.org/person/celli-horstman)**
Senior Research Associate, Delivery System Reform, The Commonwealth Fund 
*   ![Image 11: Corinne Lewis](https://www.commonwealthfund.org/sites/default/files/styles/bio_photo_thumb_160x160_/public/CL_700x700.png?itok=lP-hQU8x)  **[Corinne Lewis](https://www.commonwealthfund.org/person/corinne-lewis)**
Assistant Vice President, Delivery System Reform, The Commonwealth Fund 

Toplines

*   Community health centers provide essential primary care to more than 31 million people, including uninsured people, people with low incomes, and people who live in rural areas. However, costs have increased faster than revenues

[](https://www.linkedin.com/shareArticle?mini=true&url=https%3A%2F%2Fwww.commonwealthfund.org%2Fblog%2F2024%2Fcommunity-health-centers-are-serving-more-patients-ever-financial-challenges-loom-large&text=Community%20health%20centers%20provide%20essential%20primary%20care%20to%20more%20than%2031%20million%20people,%20including%20uninsured%20people,%20people%20with%20low%20incomes,%20and%20people%20who%20live%20in%20rural%20areas.%20However,%20costs%20have%20increased%20faster%20than%20revenues) 
*   A raft of financial pressures has put community health centers (CHCs) in a precarious position, with narrow or negative profit margins

[](https://www.linkedin.com/shareArticle?mini=true&url=https%3A%2F%2Fwww.commonwealthfund.org%2Fblog%2F2024%2Fcommunity-health-centers-are-serving-more-patients-ever-financial-challenges-loom-large&text=A%20raft%20of%20financial%20pressures%20has%20put%20community%20health%20centers%20(CHCs)%20in%20a%20precarious%20position,%20with%20narrow%20or%20negative%20profit%20margins) 

Related Content

*   [Community Health Centers’ Progress and Challenges in Meeting Patients’ Essential Primary Care Needs](https://www.commonwealthfund.org/publications/issue-briefs/2024/aug/community-health-centers-meeting-primary-care-needs-2024-FQHC-survey)
*   [Community Health Centers Need Increased and Sustained Federal Funding](https://www.commonwealthfund.org/blog/2023/community-health-centers-need-increased-and-sustained-federal-funding)
*   [Underfunded and Overburdened: The Toll of the COVID-19 Pandemic on Community Health Centers](https://www.commonwealthfund.org/blog/2023/underfunded-and-overburdened-toll-covid-19-pandemic-community-health-centers)

Community health centers (CHCs) are providing high-quality, comprehensive primary care to more patients than ever. In 2023, CHCs served a [record-setting 31 million patients](https://bphc.hrsa.gov/sites/default/files/bphc/data-reporting/2023-uds-trends-data-brief.pdf), including more than 19 million people with incomes below 200 percent of the federal poverty level (i.e., $29,160 per year for an individual in 2023), 5.6 million people without insurance, and 9.7 million people living in rural areas. As a result, CHCs’ costs are rising — but their funding and revenues haven’t kept pace.

To better understand their financial pressures, we analyzed data from the Commonwealth Fund [2024 Survey of Federally Qualified Health Centers](https://www.commonwealthfund.org/publications/issue-briefs/2024/aug/community-health-centers-meeting-primary-care-needs-2024-FQHC-survey), as well as new national [community health center data](https://data.hrsa.gov/tools/data-reporting/program-data/national). We found that CHCs have been experiencing financial challenges for several years, with some concerned their financial stability will worsen over the next two years.

Community Health Centers Face Mounting Financial Challenges
-----------------------------------------------------------

CHCs are funded through [various revenue sources](https://www.commonwealthfund.org/blog/2023/community-health-centers-need-increased-and-sustained-federal-funding), including insurance reimbursements, federal grants and appropriations, and revenue from the 340B program, which allows safety-net providers to purchase drugs at discounted rates and use the savings to cover other services. Half of CHC patients are covered by Medicaid and these reimbursements make up CHCs’ largest revenue source. Another significant portion of CHC patients ([nearly 18% in 2023](https://data.hrsa.gov/tools/data-reporting/program-data/national/table?tableName=4&year=2023)) are uninsured; CHCs depend on federal grants to cover the cost of providing uncompensated care.

Across sources, CHCs face threats that impact their financial stability. For years, relatively low Medicaid reimbursement rates and [stagnant federal funding](https://www.nachc.org/wp-content/uploads/2023/07/Overlooked-Decline-Community-Health-Center-Funding_2023_Full-report.pdf) have forced CHCs to operate on thin margins. More recently, declining Medicaid enrollment caused by nationwide [Medicaid eligibility redeterminations](https://www.commonwealthfund.org/blog/2023/almost-38-million-people-have-lost-their-medicaid-coverage-end-covid-19-public-health), the expiration of temporary [COVID-era federal funding](https://www.kff.org/medicaid/issue-brief/recent-trends-in-community-health-center-patients-services-and-financing/#:~:text=Additionally%2C%20pandemic%2Drelated%20funding%20made,patients%20and%20COVID%2Drelated%20funding.), and [restrictions from drug manufacturers](https://www.commonwealthfund.org/blog/2023/community-health-centers-need-increased-and-sustained-federal-funding) on 340B discounts have exacerbated CHCs’ financial challenges. CHCs are also grappling with uncertainty over the timing and generosity of future federal investments. The Community Health Center Fund, which [provides 70 percent](https://www.nachc.org/resource/federal-health-center-funding-101/) of CHCs’ federal funding, is set to expire at the end of 2024.

![Image 12: Community health centers’ financial margins narrowed substantially between 2021 and 2023](https://www.commonwealthfund.org/sites/default/files/community-health-centers-are-serving-more-patients-than-ever-but-financial-challenges-loom-large-exhibit-1%20%281%29.png)

Altogether, these pressures leave CHCs in a precarious position, with the narrowest national margins since 2020. Overall, there was a 64 percent decline in profit margins between 2021 and 2023. If trends continue, [experts estimate](https://geigergibson.publichealth.gwu.edu/72-community-health-centers-grew-through-2023-serious-hazards-are-horizon) that in 2024 costs will ultimately exceed revenues. While this doesn’t mean that every CHC will operate on negative margins, it signals that CHCs are struggling to keep up with rising costs as the [volume of patient visits](https://www.hrsa.gov/about/news/press-releases/new-data-health-center-patients) increases. A recent analysis of CHCs’ [costs and revenues](https://geigergibson.publichealth.gwu.edu/72-community-health-centers-grew-through-2023-serious-hazards-are-horizon) confirms this. In 2023, nearly half of CHCs had negative margins and overall net margins were 1.6 percent, the lowest level since 2020.

Underfunding and narrow margins leave CHCs with little room to make improvements, innovate, or address ongoing challenges, like competitively recruiting staff, adding new services, or investing in technology or infrastructure — all of which impact low-income patients’ access to and quality of care.

![Image 13: Nearly a quarter of health centers expect financial stability to decrease over the next two years](https://www.commonwealthfund.org/sites/default/files/community-health-centers-are-serving-more-patients-than-ever-but-financial-challenges-loom-large-exhibit-2.png)

Data from the Commonwealth Fund 2024 National Survey of Federally Qualified Health Centers reveal that in the coming years, many CHC leaders expect their financial challenges to persist or worsen. They are particularly concerned about the effects that declining Medicaid enrollment, rising levels of uncompensated care, and changes to the 340B program will have on finances.

Findings from the survey include:

**CHCs report mixed projections of financial stability over the next two years**. Nearly one-quarter of CHCs (24%) expect their financial stability to decrease in the next two years; one-half expect no changes. Research confirms that most CHCs already operate on low margins, so reports of unchanged financial stability may still indicate financial stress.

**Nearly one of five CHCs (19%) expect Medicaid revenues to decrease; nearly two of five (42%) expect uncompensated care to increase in the next two years**. The projected decline in Medicaid revenue likely stems from Medicaid eligibility redeterminations, which have drastically reduced the number of people covered by Medicaid. As of April 2024 — one year into the redeterminations process — nearly one of four [CHC patients on Medicaid](https://www.nachc.org/resource/the-impact-of-medicaid-redetermination-on-community-health-center-patients-revenue-and-resources/) had been disenrolled and few had reenrolled. CHCs reported average Medicaid revenue losses of nearly $600,000 per center because of these coverage losses. As a result of the Medicaid losses, uninsured rates are rising and will likely lead to CHCs providing more uncompensated care than in recent years.

**Thirty-eight percent of CHCs expect revenues from the 340B program to decrease in the next two years**. Since 2020, many drug manufacturers have restricted the discounts available to providers through the 340B program, which limits the savings CHCs and other safety-net providers can generate. These restrictions have been the subject of ongoing litigation, although they have continued after a [2023 federal court ruling](https://www.statnews.com/pharmalot/2023/01/30/hospitals-pharmaceuticals-340b-pharmacies-appeal/). CHC leaders have reported this policy change is already taking a toll on their finances.

Policy Opportunities to Improve CHCs’ Financial Stability
---------------------------------------------------------

Federal and state policymakers can take steps to improve CHCs’ financial stability, including:

*   Increasing federal funding to offset CHCs’ rising uncompensated care costs and support needed investments in infrastructure and technology. Congress can alleviate CHCs’ current funding uncertainty by reauthorizing the Community Health Center Fund at an increased level for multiple years before it expires at the end of 2024
*   Stabilizing Medicaid coverage by simplifying [application and renewal](https://www.kff.org/policy-watch/understanding-medicaid-ex-parte-renewals-during-the-unwinding/) processes and promoting [continuous enrollment](https://www.commonwealthfund.org/blog/2023/states-are-adopting-new-policies-help-children-stay-enrolled-medicaid-and-chip).
*   Providing additional opportunities for CHCs to participate in value-based payment models, which offer more predictable, flexible funding and reward the provision of high-quality, comprehensive care.
*   Addressing revenue losses caused by recent changes to the 340B program, either by reforming the program or providing supplemental payments to CHCs.

enriched_points.expanded_content: Community health centers face severe funding constraints due to stagnant federal financing, increasing operational costs, and reliance on diminishing revenue sources.
A diminishing workforce exacerbates challenges in community-based healthcare, with recruitment and retention hindered by low pay and high workload demands.
Engaging communities effectively is often hindered by mistrust and lack of awareness about available services, which impedes the adoption of healthcare programs.
High turnover rates among healthcare staff disrupt continuity of care, leading to increased operational costs and negatively impacting patient relationships and trust.
